Parameter.Description: Parameter: Basis x.

Parameter.Name: y
Parameter.Description: Parameter: Exponent y.

Parameter.Name: x^y
Parameter.Description: Result: Returned exponential value (x^y).

Statement.Description: Returns a string containing the date and time in a format you specify using the time format string parameter. The format codes for the time format string are listed in the following table. The format of Time String is defined by the FormatString. If FormatString is empty the format: %Y/%m/%d %H:%M:%S is assumed by default. If add msec is TRUE the millisecond value is appended to the end of the string.
Statement.Name: GetDateTimeString

Parameter.Name: time format string
Parameter.Description: Parameter: The format of Time String is defined by the FormatString. If FormatString is empty the format: %Y/%m/%d %H:%M:%S is assumed by default. If add msec is TRUE the millisecond value is appended to the end of the string. Format Code: Value for FormatString; %%: a single percent character; %a: abbreviated weekday name (e.g. Wed); %A: full weekday name (e.g. Wednesday); %b: abbreviated month name (e.g. Jun); %B: full month name (e.g. June); %c: locale?s default date and time representation; %d: day of month (01 ? 31); %H: hour (24-hour clock) (00 ? 23); %I: hour (12-hour clock) (01 ? 12); %j: day number of year (001 ? 366); %m: month number (01 ? 12); %M: minute (00 ? 59); %p: AM or PM flag; %S: seconds (00 ? 59); %U: week number of the year (00 ? 53), with the first Sunday as the first day of week one; %w: weekday as a decimal number (0 ? 6), with 0 representing Sunday; %W: week number of the year (00 ? 53), with the first Monday as the first day of week one; %x: locale?s date representation; %X: locale?s time representation; %y: year within century (00 ? 99); %Y: year, including the century (for example, 1997); %Z: time zone name or abbreviation.

Parameter.Name: date/time string
Parameter.Description: Result: Returns the time string of the given time Seconds (seconds since 1 Jan 1904). If Seconds is empty or zero the actual system time is returned.

Statement.Description: Returns IP-Address of a given computer name. If the address returned should be in format with dots (e.g. 192.168.0.87), set DotNotation to ON.

Parameter.Description: The resolved IP address

Parameter.Name: Path out

Parameter.Description: Result: Number of bytes of string.

Statement.Description: This statement is used in combination with the IfTimeout statement to achieve a multi-sequencer capable timeout facility. GetTimer returns an initial timer value (in ms). IfTimeout checks whether the expired time period since TimerStart (pass the value from GetTimer!) is greater than Timeout [ms]. If TRUE, the next sequence statement is executed (normally a Goto to an error handling part of the sequence), else the next statement is skipped.
Statement.Name: GetTimer

Parameter.Name: Timer val
Parameter.Description: Result: Returns timer value.

Statement.Description: Returns the user level of the actual logged in user.
Statement.Name: GetUserLevel

Parameter.Name: UserLevelName
Parameter.Description: Name of the user level of the actual logged in user. The following names are returned and correspond to the user level: 0 = no access 1 = operator 2 = setup 3 = service 4 = super admin

Parameter.Name: UserLevel
Parameter.Description: User level of the actual logged in user. 0 = no access 1 = operator 2 = setup 3 = service 4 = super admin

Statement.Description: Returns name of current user.
Statement.Name: GetUserName

Parameter.Name: User
Parameter.Description: Result: Returns user name.

Statement.Description: This statement compares two numeric values, if condition is TRUE statement in line is executed, if condition is FALSE next line is skipped.

Parameter.Description: Parameter: Enter first sting.

Parameter.Name: Comp
Parameter.Description: Parameter: Select condition from list (= or <>).

Parameter.Name: String2
Parameter.Description: Parameter: Enter second string.

Statement.Description: This statement is used in combination with GetTimer to achieve a multi-sequencer capable timeout facility.GetTimer returns an initial timer value (in ms). IfTimeout checks, whether the expired period since TimerStart (pass the value from GetTimer!) is greater than Timeout [ms]. If TRUE, the next statement is executed (normally a Goto to an error handling part of the sequence), else the next statement is skipped.
Statement.Name: IfTimeout

Parameter.Name: TimerStart
Parameter.Description: Parameter: Enter start time.

Parameter.Name: Timeout [ms]
Parameter.Description: Parameter: Enter time until timeout.

Statement.Description: This statement opens a dialog which prompts the user for input. The text 'Dialog text' is displayed. Initially, the text 'Default text' is displayed in the edit field. The result is returned as both string (in 'Output') and float point number (in 'Numeric output').

Statement.Description: Rebuilds the sub-sequence and sequence cache in context of the currently defined sub-sequence search paths. The sub-sequence search paths define the locations where sub-sequences and sequences are located/searched. These search paths can be defined in the TestMaster environment. Every sequence and sub-sequence found at these location(s) is added to the corresponding cache that provides faster and deterministic access.
Statement.Name: RebuildCache

Parameter.Name: Show dialog
Parameter.Description: Enables (1) or disables (0) the progress visualization. Rebuilding many sequences can result in a moderate waiting time the user should be informed about. On a Windows system the progress is visualized through the TestMaster progress bar. On a real time (RT) system a user interface is not present. In this situation the currently cached sequence number is printed to the corresponding console.

Statement.Description: Resolve a path from an SM-symbolic or absolute path. The requiered lookup direction is determined automaticly.
Statement.Name: ResolvePath

Parameter.Name: Path in
Parameter.Description: Path to resolve. Absolute path will be returned as SM-symbolic path. A SM-symbolic path will be returned as an absolute path

Parameter.Name: Path out
Parameter.Description: The resolved path

Parameter.Name: Valid
Parameter.Description: Returns true if the path is not empty AND not an invalid path

Statement.Description: Searches for regular expression in string beginning at offset. If it finds a match, it splits the string into the three substrings: before substring, match substring, after substring.
Statement.Name: SearchString

Parameter.Name: regular expression
Parameter.Description: Parameter: Enter regular expression to be found. Special Characters are interpreted as: .: Matches any character; ?: Matches zero or one instances of the expression preceding. ? cancels the interpretation of special characters (for example, \? matches a question mark). You can also use the following constructions for the space and non displayable characters:\b backspace\f form feed\n new line\s space\r carriage return\xx any character, where xx is the hex code using 0 through 9 and upper case A through F\t tab; ^: if ^ is the first character of regular expression, it anchors the match to the offset in string. The match fails unless regular expression matches that topic of string that begins with the character at offset. If ^ is not the first character, it is treated as a regular character; [ ]: Encloses alternates (for example, [abc] matches a, b, or c. The following character has special significance when used within the brackets in the following manner), - (dash) Indicates a range when used between digits, or lowercase or uppercase letters (for example, [0-5],[a-g], or [L-Q]), the following characters have significance only when they are the first character within the brackets.~ Excludes the set of characters, including non displayable characters. [~0-9] matches any character other than 0 through 9. ^ Excludes the set with respect to all the displayable characters (and the space characters). [^0-9] gives the space characters and all displayable characters except 0 through 9; +: Matches the longest number of instances of the expression preceding +; there must be at least one instance to constitute a match; *: matches the longest number of instances of the expression preceding +; there must be at least one instance to constitute a match; $: if $ is the last character of regular expression, it anchors the match to the last element of string. The match fails unless regular expression matches up to and including the last character in the string. If $ is not last, it is treated as a regular character. Examples for search string are: VOLTS: VOLTS; All uppercase and lowercase versions of volts, that is, VOLTS, Volts, volts, and so on: [Vv][Oo][Ll][Tt][Ss; A space, a plus sign, or a minus sign: [ +-]; A sequence of one or more digits: [0-9]+; Zero or more Spaces: \s* or * (that is, a space followed by an asterisk); One or more Spaces, Tabs, New lines, or Carriage Returns: [\t \r \n \s]+; One or more characters other than digits: [~0-9]+; The word Level only if it begins at the offset position in the string: ^Level; The word Volts only if it appears at the end of the string: Volts$; The longest string within parentheses: (.*); The longest string within parentheses but not containing any parentheses within it: ([~( )]*); The character [: [ [ ]"

Parameter.Name: string
Parameter.Description: Parameter: Enter string to be to be searched; Examples vor search string are: VOLTS: VOLTS; All uppercase and lowercase versions of volts, that is, VOLTS, Volts, volts, and so on: [Vv][Oo][Ll][Tt][Ss]; A space, a plus sign, or a minus sign: [ +-]; A sequence of one or more digits: [0-9]+; Zero or more Spaces \s* or * (that is, a space followed by an asterisk); One or more Spaces, Tabs, New lines, or Carriage Returns: [\t \r \n \s]+; One or more characters other than digits: [~0-9]+; The word Level only if it begins at the offset position in the string: ^Level; The word Volts only if it appears at the end of the string: VOLTS:VOLTS; All uppercase and lowercase versions of volts, that is, VOLTS, Volts, volts, and so on: [Vv][Oo][Ll][Tt][Ss]; A space, a plus sign, or a minus sign: [ +-]; A sequence of one or more digits: [0-9]+; Zero or more Spaces: \s* or * (that is, a space followed by an asterisk); One or more Spaces, Tabs, New lines, or Carriage Returns: [\t \r \n \s]+: One or more characters other than digits: [~0-9]+:The word Level only if it begins at the offset position in the string: ^Level; The word Volts only if it appears at the end of the string:Volts$; The longest string within parentheses: (.*); The longest string within parentheses but not containing any parentheses within it: ([~( )]*): The character: [ [ [ ].

Parameter.Name: offset
Parameter.Description: Parameter: Numbers of characters to skip.

Parameter.Name: before substring
Parameter.Description: Result: Part of expression before find string.

Parameter.Name: match substring
Parameter.Description: Results: Matched string.

Parameter.Name: after substring
Parameter.Description: Result: Part of expression behind find string.

Parameter.Name: offset past match
Parameter.Description: Result: Offset past match is the index of the first character after the match string. If no match is found offset past match is set to -1.

Statement.Description: Executes a system command. Use the System Exec VI to execute or launch other Windows?based applications, command-line applications, (Windows) batch files, or (macOS and Linux) script files from within VIs. With the System Exec VI, you can include any parameters within your command string that the executing command supports.
Statement.Name: SystemExecute

Parameter.Name: command line
Parameter.Description: "command line" indicates the command LabVIEW calls to run a program. It is often usefull to use the prefix "cmd /c " before your call to close the CMD after execution.

Parameter.Name: standard input
Parameter.Description: "standard input" is the text to pass to the command line as standard input.

Parameter.Name: working directory
Parameter.Description: "working directory" is the file system directory from which you want to execute the command. You do not have to set "working directory".

Parameter.Name: wait until completion?
Parameter.Description: If "wait until completion?" is TRUE, "standard input" is available as input to the command and "standard output" and "standard error" are available when the command finishes running. If FALSE, the command runs in the background and its input and output are not available.

Parameter.Name: run minimized?
Parameter.Description: If "run minimized?" is TRUE, the VI minimizes the run of your executable program.

Parameter.Name: expected output size
Parameter.Description: Use "expected output size" to improve memory efficiency. Use a number slightly larger than the output size you expect. The command runs if you exceed this size, but LabVIEW uses its memory less efficiently. The default is 4096.

Parameter.Name: standard error
Parameter.Description: "standard error" returns errors from the program you called if "wait until completion" is TRUE. If "wait until completion" is FALSE, System Exec returns an empty string.

Parameter.Name: standard output
Parameter.Description: "standard output" returns information from the program you called if "wait until completion" is TRUE. If "wait until completion" is FALSE, System Exec returns an empty string.

Parameter.Name: return code
Parameter.Description: "return code" indicates the system-dependent exit code that the command returns.

Statement.Description: Returns the time string of the given time SECONDS (seconds since 1 Jan 1904). If SECONDS is empty or zero the actual system time is returned. The format of Time String is defined by the FORMATSTRING. If FORMATSTRING is empty the format: %Y/%m/%d %H:%M:%S is assumed by default. If add msec is TRUE the millisecond value is appended to the end of the string.
Statement.Name: TimeFormat

Parameter.Name: FormatString
Parameter.Description: Parameter: The format of Time String is defined by the FormatString. If FormatString is empty the format: %Y/%m/%d %H:%M:%S is assumed by default. If add msec is TRUE the millisecond value is appended to the end of the string. Format Code: Value for FormatString; %%: a single percent character; %a: abbreviated weekday name (e.g. Wed); %A: full weekday name (e.g. Wednesday); %b: abbreviated month name (e.g. Jun); %B: full month name (e.g. June); %c: locale?s default date and time representation; %d: day of month (01 ? 31); %H: hour (24-hour clock) (00 ? 23); %I: hour (12-hour clock) (01 ? 12); %j: day number of year (001 ? 366); %m: month number (01 ? 12); %M: minute (00 ? 59); %p: AM or PM flag; %S: seconds (00 ? 59); %U: week number of the year (00 ? 53), with the first Sunday as the first day of week one; %w: weekday as a decimal number (0 ? 6), with 0 representing Sunday; %W: week number of the year (00 ? 53), with the first Monday as the first day of week one; %x: locale?s date representation; %X: locale?s time representation; %y: year within century (00 ? 99); %Y: year, including the century (for example, 1997); %Z: time zone name or abbreviation.

Parameter.Name: add msec
Parameter.Description: Parameter: Adds .%3d msec to time string.

Parameter.Name: Seconds
Parameter.Description: Parameter: If Seconds = 0: current time, if seconds <> 0 seconds are formatted to time string.

Parameter.Name: TimeString
Parameter.Description: Result: The format of TIMESTRING is defined by the FORMAT STRING. If FORMATSTRING is empty the format: %Y/%m/%d %H:%M:%S is assumed by default. f add msec is TRUE the millisecond value is appended to the end of the string.

Statement.Description: This statement activates the UserDialog ControlPanel with the selected dialog_ini file. All *.dlg files generated with the UserDialog Control Panel in administrator mode available in the actual process folder are available for selection. The user inputs will be placed in the process_var.ini file for recall by the GetNumVar/GetStringVar statements for the variables with the exact names of the corresponding input field. This mechanisms allows a secure and versatile possibility for the input of dynamic user parameters into a running process. The configuration of the input form can be performed by the interactive use of the UserDataInputDialog. The user data input dialogue is a variable input dialogue form, which is suitable when multiple user data inputs are requested at a point in a process or test sequence, e.g. identification of a device prior to processing. The dialogue has two operational modes: User mode and Admin Mode. The admin mode will allow to define which field, meaning and values are requested or allowed. The user mode will just request the input of the values and not allow any further change of the dialogue Input fields can be configured to expect specific types of input (numeric, alphanumeric) and the input into a field can be optional or requested. Another configuration option allows to fill up a field with default values or alternatively the last value entered. The defined settings can be stored while in admin mode to a parameter *.dlg-file for use by the UserDialog-Statement from within a sequence.
Statement.Name: UserDialog

Parameter.Name: dialog SET
Parameter.Description: Parameter: Select Dialog_ini_file from drop down list.

Statement.Description: This statement activates the UserDialog ControlPanel with the selected dialog_ini file. All *.dlg files generated with the UserDialog Control Panel in administrator mode available in the actual process folder are available for selection. The user inputs will be placed in the process_var.ini file for recall by the GetNumVar/GetStringVar statements for the variables with the exact names of the corresponding input field. This mechanisms allows a secure and versatile possibility for the input of dynamic user parameters into a running process. The configuration of the input form can be performed by the interactive use of the UserDataInputDialog. The user data input dialogue is a variable input dialogue form, which is suitable when multiple user data inputs are requested at a point in a process or test sequence, e.g. identification of a device prior to processing. The dialogue has two operational modes: User mode and Admin Mode. The admin mode will allow to define which field, meaning and values are requested or allowed. The user mode will just request the input of the values and not allow any further change of the dialogue Input fields can be configured to expect specific types of input (numeric, alphanumeric) and the input into a field can be optional or requested. Another configuration option allows to fill up a field with default values or alternatively the last value entered. The defined settings can be stored while in admin mode to a parameter *.dlg-file for use by the UserDialog-Statement from within a sequence.
Statement.Name: UserDialogModal

Parameter.Name: dialog SET
Parameter.Description: Parameter: Select Dialog_ini_file from drop down list.

Statement.Description: Defines that the subsequence is asynchronous executable. As a result, prototypes are made available for the corresponding subsequence. These additional prototypes allow an asynchronously start (StartAsync) and wait on a finished (WaitOnAsync) sequence. These prototypes follow the naming scheme "SEQ::StartAsync::SUB_<sequence name>" and "SEQ::WaitOnAsync::SUB_<sequence name>".
Statement.Name: DeclareAsAsync

Statement.Description: Defines a subsequence as synchronous. As a result, a prototype is made available for the subsequence, that allows it to be executed synchronously. The prototype follows the naming scheme "SEQ::SUB_ <sequence name>"
Statement.Name: DeclareAsSync

Statement.Description: Defines a numeric parameter value to be used in other sequences or subsequences. The statement requires definition of the parameter name and the numeric value.
Statement.Name: DeclareNumParam

Parameter.Name: Name
Parameter.Description: Name (label) parameter.

Parameter.Name: Default value
Parameter.Description: Enter default value for parameter in numeric format.

Parameter.Name: Value
Parameter.Description: If the sequence is running stand alone ? not called from higher instance ? the value is = Default value; if the sequence is called from another sequence / subsequence, the Value is defined by the calling sequence.

Statement.Description: Defines a parameter value as string to be used in other sequences or subsequences. The statement requires definition of the parameter name and the string value.
Statement.Name: DeclareStrParam

Parameter.Name: Name
Parameter.Description: Name (label) parameter.

Parameter.Name: Default value
Parameter.Description: Enter default value for parameter in string format.

Parameter.Name: Value
Parameter.Description: If the sequence is running stand alone ? not called from higher instance ? the value is = Default value; if the sequence is called from another sequence / subsequence, the Value is defined by the calling sequence.

Statement.Description: Allows to define a value selectable from a table of values ? e.g. IOPoints or devices ? to be used in other sequences or subsequences. The statement requires the name of the tab file, the selected list entry and the default value. The list has to be available as tab delimited .txt file and has to be place in the process folder. The list entries are passed as strings.
Statement.Name: DeclareTypedParam

Parameter.Name: Name
Parameter.Description: Name (label) parameter.

Parameter.Name: Type
Parameter.Description: Enter type from list.

Parameter.Name: Default value
Parameter.Description: Enter default value for parameter in string format.

Parameter.Name: Value
Parameter.Description: If the sequence is running stand alone ? not called from higher instance ? the value is = Default value; if the sequence is called from another sequence / subsequence, the Value is defined by the calling sequence.

Statement.Description: Allows to return a numeric value from the a subsequence in the calling sequence. The statement requires the parameter name and value as input.
Statement.Name: ReturnNumParam

Parameter.Name: Name
Parameter.Description: Name (label) parameter.

Parameter.Name: Value
Parameter.Description: Enter default value for parameter in numeric format.

Statement.Description: Allows to return a string value from the a subsequence in the calling sequence. The statement requires the parameter name and value as input.
Statement.Name: ReturnStrParam

Parameter.Name: Name
Parameter.Description: Name (label) parameter.

Parameter.Name: Value
Parameter.Description: Enter default value for parameter in string format.

Statement.Description: Allows to define a numeric parameter value to be used in other sequences or subsequences. The statement requires definition of the parameter name and the numeric value in the calling sequence. This statement requires the DeclareNumParam in the called sequence.
Statement.Name: SetNumParam

Parameter.Name: Name
Parameter.Description: Name (label) parameter.

Parameter.Name: Value
Parameter.Description: Enter default value for parameter in number format.

Statement.Description: Allows to define a string parameter value to be used in other sequences or subsequences. The statement requires definition of the parameter name and the string value in the calling sequence. This statement requires the DeclareStrParam in the called sequence.
Statement.Name: SetStrParam

Parameter.Name: Name
Parameter.Description: Name (label) parameter.

Parameter.Name: Value
Parameter.Description: Enter default value for parameter in string format.

Statement.Description: Clears a jump target that was previously set with the OnError statement.
Statement.Name: ClearOnError

Statement.Description: Returns an error code as numerical value and an error string as text. Error code and error text are defined by the PushError statement. This statement requires the PushError statement before.
Statement.Name: GetError

Parameter.Name: Error code
Parameter.Description: Error code defined by PushError.

Parameter.Name: Error text
Parameter.Description: Error text defined by PushError.

Statement.Description: Allows jump to a before defined label in case an error occurs. This statement requires the PushError statement before.
Statement.Name: OnError

Parameter.Name: Label
Parameter.Description: Enter name of label to jump to.

Statement.Description: Returns the last pushed error from the error storage and terminates the execution. A dialogue will show the error message. This statement requires the PushError statement before. Note: The sequencer will terminate without any error if no error message is available in the error storage (e.g. was pushed).
Statement.Name: PopError

Statement.Description: Pushes the error condition to the error storage. Execution continues normally. This error can be checked in the following sequence by PopError.
Statement.Name: PushError

Parameter.Name: Return code
Parameter.Description: Enter return code.

Parameter.Name: Message
Parameter.Description: Enter error message.

Statement.Description: Executes a sub-sequence, and resumes execution afterwards with the next sequence step. The called sub-sequence can be specified in two ways: * by a full path: The sequence can be found at this exact location in the file hierarchy *by a sequence name: the sequencer searches in the following order for a sequence with the given name. 1. in the path of the current sequence; 2. system sequence path for the sequence to execute.
Statement.Name: ExecSeq

Parameter.Name: Sequence
Parameter.Description: Select sequence by the opening file dialogue.

Statement.Description: Exectues a sequence on a remote target computer cofnigured in the system configuration. The sequence is defined by its name. The path is determined by the target system configuration (process, system...). The execution can be performed synchronously (Wait until completion = 1) or asynchronously (=0). The relative priority of the sequence execution on the target can be defined by a positive number between 0 and 65535). The CPU core of execution can be set by a positive numeric value defining the selected core or -2 allowing the scheduler to select the core.
Statement.Name: ExecSeqOnTarget

Parameter.Name: Target
Parameter.Description: Name of the execution target

Parameter.Name: Sequence
Parameter.Description: Full Name of the sequence to be executed. Based on the name the sequence path will be resolved in the file hierarchy of the target system following the TestMaster logic and the defined search paths.

Parameter.Name: Wait until completion
Parameter.Description: Execution mode for this statement. 0: start sequence on target and continue with next statement immediately 1: start sequence on remote target and continue with next statement, when the called sequence has completed execution

Parameter.Name: Priority
Parameter.Description: Relative execution priorotiy of the sequence. The priority is a positive number between 0 and 65535. The higher the number the more priority is given to this sequence execution compared to other sequences with a lower number. A good value is 100.

Parameter.Name: Processor (-2 auto)
Parameter.Description: Select a CPU core for the execution of the sequence. The number must be between 0 and the number of cores -1. A value of -2 leaves the core selection to the scheduler (auto selection). -1 is invalid

Parameter.Name: TargetSequencePath
Parameter.Description: File path of the executed sequence in the rremote target file system

Parameter.Name: TargetSequenceUUID
Parameter.Description: Unique identifier of the sequence execution on the remote targett. This parameter can be used to chekc for the sequence state of an asynchronousexecuted sequence.

Parameter.Name: TargetReference
Parameter.Description: Internal reference to the remote target which can be used with subsequent statements, e.g. to check for the state of a asynchronous sequence execution.

Statement.Description: This statement tests for a delayed sequence abort request. This is useful for endless sequence loops, which the user wants to abort only at special, safe locations, i.e. a cleaning loop which runs overnight.
Statement.Name: TestAbort

Statement.Description: This is the Comment statement. It can be used to insert comments into the sequences for documentation purposes. It has only one parameter, the text comment. During execution, sequence steps with a **** statement are ignored.

Parameter.Name: Comment
Parameter.Description: Enter comment text.

Statement.Description: Starts a sequence part which requires the end tag End. The Begin... End structure is e.g. used in if...else structures to mark conditions.
Statement.Name: BEGIN

Statement.Description: Delays the execution of the sequence by the specified number of milliseconds.
Statement.Name: Delay

Parameter.Name: Wait time [ms]
Parameter.Description: Enter delay in milliseconds.

Statement.Description: ELSE is part of a structure, which requires one of the if conditions ifnum or ifstring before. ifnum and ifstring can be found in the standard statements directory.
Statement.Name: ELSE

Statement.Description: Ends a sequence part, which has the start tag Begin. The Begin ... End structure is e.g. used in if... ELSE constructions.
Statement.Name: END

Statement.Description: The EndFor statement requires For in a For loop. Endfor has to be inserted after the last line of an action to be repeated, no further parameters have to be defined.
Statement.Name: ENDFOR

Statement.Description: EndWhile requires While at the beginning of a loop. EndWhile has to be inserted after the last line of the action to be repeated, no further parameters have to be defined.
Statement.Name: ENDWHILE

Statement.Description:
FOR has to be inserted before the first line to be repeated. The loop is closed by the 'EndFor' statement. All enclosed statements are repeated until 'variable' reaches 'end' value. The first iteration updates 'variable' to the 'start' value. The loop is executed until 'variable' reaches the 'end' value, but at least once. The iterator is incremented by the value of 'step' each repetition.
Notice: Make sure your iterator can reach the end condition or the sequence will loop indefinitely
Statement.Name: FOR

Parameter.Name: Variable
Parameter.Description: Name of the variable.

Parameter.Name: Start
Parameter.Description: Start value.

Parameter.Name: End
Parameter.Description: End value.

Parameter.Name: Step
Parameter.Description: Step size value.

Statement.Description: Searches for the label (1st column in the sequence editor) that matches its single argument exactly. If found sequence execution is resumed with the labelled step.
Statement.Name: Goto

Parameter.Name: Label
Parameter.Description: Enter name of label to jump to.

Statement.Description: Repeat is part of the repeat ... until structure, which allows to repeat a part of a sequence until a defined condition is reached. The sequence part is performed only one time. This statement requires the Until statement to complete the operation.
Statement.Name: REPEAT

Statement.Description: Until is the second part of the repeat ... until structure, which allows to repeat a part of a sequence until a defined condition is reached. The sequence part is performed only one time. This statement requires the Repeat statement as start tag before to complete the operation.
Statement.Name: UNTIL

Parameter.Name: Value1
Parameter.Description: First value to be compared.

Parameter.Name: Operator2
Parameter.Description: Value of string type.

Statement.Description: While has to be inserted before the first line of while loop. A state has to be defined by a operator (<, <=, =, >=, >, <>) and a value of string type. While requires EndWhile at the end of the loop.
Statement.Name: WHILE

Parameter.Name: Value1
Parameter.Description: First value to be compared.

Parameter.Name: Operator2
Parameter.Description: Value of string type.

Statement.Description: Starts a sequence trace for the current sequence. The trace is stored in a global common trace log.
Statement.Name: StartSequenceTrace

Statement.Description: Stops a sequence trace for the current sequence.
Statement.Name: StopSequenceTrace
